section.title: The Reign of Jotham
section.sectionRationale: Jotham learns the lesson his father Uzziah paid for so dearly: he does what is right in the eyes of the LORD, but does not enter the temple of the LORD himself. His reign is marked by building projects, military victory over the Ammonites, and quiet, steady strength, because he orders his ways before the LORD his God, even while the people around him continue to behave corruptly.
section.thesis: Jotham's brief, understated reign shows that a king can learn from a parent's specific failure without inheriting it, and that ordering one's ways before the LORD produces real strength even in an age when the wider people's hearts remain corrupt.
section.frameNote: Two beats: Jotham's righteous reign and careful respect for priestly boundaries, verses 1 to 6; and a summary of his building, his victory over Ammon, and his death, verses 7 to 9.

units.label: Jotham reigns in Judah
units.note: he did not enter the temple of the LORD

verses.text: Jotham was twenty-five years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem sixteen years. His mother's name was Jerushah daughter of Zadok.
And he did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, just as his father Uzziah had done. In addition, he did not enter the temple of the LORD. But the people still behaved corruptly.
Jotham rebuilt the Upper Gate of the house of the LORD, and he worked extensively on the wall at the hill of Ophel.
He also built cities in the hill country of Judah and fortresses and towers in the forests.

units.label: Jotham's strength and death
units.note: he ordered his ways before the LORD

verses.text: Jotham waged war against the king of the Ammonites and defeated them, and that year they gave him a hundred talents of silver, ten thousand cors of wheat, and ten thousand cors of barley. They paid him the same in the second and third years.
So Jotham grew powerful because he {{kun|ordered}} his ways before the LORD his God.
As for the rest of the acts of Jotham, along with all his wars and his ways, they are indeed written in the Book of the Kings of Israel and Judah.
He was twenty-five years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem sixteen years.
And Jotham rested with his fathers and was buried in the City of David. And his son Ahaz reigned in his place.

kun.gloss: To establish, prepare, or order rightly. Jotham grew powerful because he kun, ordered or established, his ways before the LORD; the same root describes a throne being firmly established, suggesting Jotham's careful, steady faithfulness gave his reign a stability his father's later pride had forfeited.

basic.context: Jotham reigns sixteen years and does what was right in the eyes of the LORD, just as his father Uzziah had done, but with one crucial difference the chronicler makes sure to note: he did not enter the temple of the LORD himself, learning directly from the catastrophe that struck his father when Uzziah presumed to burn incense reserved for priests. Jotham rebuilds gates and walls, fortifies the hill country, defeats the Ammonites and receives heavy tribute from them for three years, and the chronicler summarizes his strength in one line: he grew powerful because he ordered his ways before the LORD his God. Yet even under this good king, the people themselves still behaved corruptly, a quiet warning that a good king's personal faithfulness does not automatically reform an entire nation's heart.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, brief by comparison with the surrounding chapters, and its brevity is itself instructive: a life ordered rightly before God, learning the right lessons from a parent's specific failure, does not need dramatic incident to be commended. It shows that faithfulness can be quiet and steady rather than spectacular, and it does not claim that a righteous king guarantees a righteous nation.
basic.originalLanguages: The chronicler's verdict on Jotham's strength is compact but pointed: he grew powerful because he kun, ordered or firmly established, his ways before the LORD his God. The same root elsewhere describes a throne being made secure, suggesting that Jotham's careful, deliberate faithfulness, in contrast to his father's presumption, gave his reign real and lasting stability.
basic.typology: Jotham's careful refusal to enter the temple himself, honoring the boundary his father violated, anticipates the pattern of a king who rightly honors the distinct role of the priesthood rather than absorbing it into his own authority, a distinction fully and rightly resolved only in Christ, who alone holds both offices without confusion or usurpation.
basic.christ: Jotham's quiet obedience, ordering his ways before the LORD and respecting the very boundary that destroyed his father, models the humility that receives correction from another's failure rather than repeating it. This points forward to Christ, the perfectly obedient Son, who, unlike every human king before Him, never once overstepped what the Father had appointed for Him, but did all things in perfect submission to the Father's will, John 5:19.
basic.god: God rewards ordered, humble faithfulness with real strength, even in a brief and otherwise unremarkable reign. Yet the chronicler's honest note that the people still behaved corruptly even under a good king shows that God does not force a nation's heart through a single ruler's righteousness alone; corporate faithfulness requires more than one faithful man at the top.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Chronicles 26:16-21 gives Uzziah's catastrophic error that Jotham learns from directly. Psalm 119:9 asks how a young man can keep his way pure, answered by keeping God's word, echoing Jotham's ordered ways. Proverbs 4:26 counsels making level paths for the feet, a similar image of ordered walking before God. John 5:19 shows Christ's perfect submission to the Father's appointed boundaries, in contrast to every human king who has overstepped them.
basic.oneStory: Jotham's brief, steady reign, marked chiefly by what he refused to do, enter the temple himself, shows that learning rightly from a predecessor's failure and ordering one's life humbly before God produces real, lasting strength, even without dramatic incident. His quiet faithfulness, alongside the honest note that the people still behaved corruptly, points forward to the one King whose perfect obedience will finally, fully reform not just a ruler's heart but an entire redeemed people.

context.literary: This is one of the briefest royal accounts in Chronicles, and its brevity itself makes a literary point: contrasted with his father's long, eventful, and ultimately tragic chapter, Jotham's understated faithfulness needs little narration, because there is little dramatic failure to record.
context.historical: The heavy Ammonite tribute, a hundred talents of silver and vast quantities of wheat and barley paid for three consecutive years, reflects Jotham's genuine military and political strength during a period when Judah still held real regional influence before the crises of his son Ahaz's reign.
context.canonical: Jotham stands as a hinge figure: the direct heir of Uzziah's tragic lesson about priestly boundaries, and the direct father of Ahaz, whose reign, covered next, will plunge Judah into its darkest idolatry yet, making Jotham's quiet faithfulness a brief respite between two more dramatic and troubled reigns.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record Jotham's genuine, quiet faithfulness, specifically his deliberate refusal to enter the temple as his father had presumptuously done, and to show that ordering one's life before the LORD produces real strength, while also honestly noting that the wider people remained corrupt even under a righteous king.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates a specific, brief historical reign; it is not suggesting that righteousness always looks understated or that a good king's personal faithfulness automatically guarantees national reform. What endures is the pattern of learning rightly and specifically from a predecessor's failure, and the sober reminder that even good leadership does not automatically transform every heart under it.

debates.question: Why does the chronicler specifically note that Jotham did not enter the temple of the LORD, when this seems like an unusual detail to single out for a king who did what was right?

views.label: A deliberate, corrective response to Uzziah's specific sin
views.case: Given that the immediately preceding chapter recounts Uzziah's catastrophic entry into the temple to burn incense, this detail reads as Jotham's conscious, careful decision to honor the very boundary his father violated, learning precisely from that specific failure.

views.label: A general statement of proper reverence and restraint before the sanctuary
views.case: Some read this more broadly as simply commending Jotham's overall humility and respect for the priesthood's distinct role, without necessarily implying a direct, deliberate reaction to his father's particular sin.

debates.bearing: Either way, the detail commends Jotham for respecting a boundary God had clearly established, offering a positive contrast to his father's presumption regardless of how consciously Jotham connected the two.

study.originalLanguages: The chronicler's summary of Jotham's strength rests on one carefully chosen verb, kun, to order, establish, or firmly set: he grew powerful because he ordered his ways before the LORD his God. This same root elsewhere describes a throne made secure, suggesting that careful, humble faithfulness, not dramatic achievement, is what actually establishes a reign.

typology.type: A king who deliberately honors the boundary between kingship and priesthood that his own father violated
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who perfectly holds together the offices of King and Priest without confusion, because He alone is rightly appointed to both, Hebrews 7:1-3, 24-25.

study.christ: Jotham's careful decision not to enter the temple himself, learning from his father's presumption, models the humility of receiving correction rather than repeating a predecessor's failure. This anticipates Christ, who, unlike every king before Him, never once overstepped what the Father had appointed, doing nothing of Himself but only what He saw the Father doing, John 5:19, and who alone rightly and fully holds together the roles of King and Priest that Uzziah wrongly tried to combine and Jotham wisely refused to confuse.
study.god: God honors quiet, ordered obedience with real strength and stability, even in a reign with little dramatic incident to record. Yet God's blessing on one faithful king does not automatically produce a faithful nation; the chronicler's honest aside, but the people still behaved corruptly, shows that corporate reform requires more than a single righteous ruler at the top.

crossReferences.note: Uzziah's catastrophic entry into the temple, the specific failure Jotham appears to have learned from directly.
How can a young man keep his way pure, by keeping it according to Your word, a fitting parallel to Jotham's ordered ways.
Make level paths for your feet, and all your ways will be established, echoing the same root used of Jotham's ordered walk.
Christ's perfect submission to the Father's appointed boundaries, the ultimate fulfillment of rightly ordered obedience.

study.oneStory: Jotham's brief, quiet reign, marked chiefly by his deliberate refusal to repeat his father's specific sin and by the strength that came from ordering his ways before the LORD, shows that faithfulness need not be dramatic to be real, though it also cannot single handedly reform an entire people's heart. His steady obedience points forward to Christ, the true King and Priest, whose perfect ordering of His ways before the Father secures not just His own reign but the redemption of all who trust in Him.

quiz.q: How did Jotham's conduct toward the temple differ from his father Uzziah's?

options.text: He did what was right just as Uzziah had, but unlike his father, he did not enter the temple of the LORD himself.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly draws this careful contrast with his father's presumptuous entry recorded in the previous chapter.

options.text: He entered the temple even more frequently than his father had.
options.note: The text explicitly states the opposite, that Jotham did not enter the temple, unlike Uzziah.

options.text: He tore down the temple and built a new one.
options.note: The text describes Jotham repairing the Upper Gate of the temple, not tearing down the structure itself.

options.text: No comparison to his father's conduct is given.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly makes this comparison to Uzziah's conduct.

quiz.q: Despite Jotham's own righteousness, what does the chronicler note about the wider people of Judah?

options.text: The people still behaved corruptly, even under his good reign.
options.note: Verse 2 gives this exact, sobering aside about the people's continued corruption.

options.text: The entire nation was fully reformed and turned to the LORD.
options.note: The text explicitly states the opposite, that the people still behaved corruptly despite Jotham's own faithfulness.

options.text: Only the priests remained corrupt while ordinary people reformed.
options.note: The text broadly states the people still behaved corruptly, without narrowing this specifically to the priesthood alone.

options.text: No comment on the people's conduct is given.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly comments on the people's continued corruption.

quiz.q: What building projects did Jotham undertake?

options.text: He rebuilt the Upper Gate of the temple, worked on the wall at Ophel, and built cities, fortresses, and towers in the hill country and forests.
options.note: Verses 3 to 4 detail these specific building achievements.

options.text: He built nothing during his entire reign.
options.note: Verses 3 to 4 explicitly describe substantial building projects across multiple locations.

options.text: He built only a single new palace for himself.
options.note: The building projects described are broader, including gates, walls, cities, and fortresses, not a single personal palace.

options.text: No building projects are mentioned in the chapter.
options.note: Verses 3 to 4 give a specific account of his building projects.

quiz.q: What military success did Jotham achieve, and what resulted from it?

options.text: He defeated the Ammonites, who then paid him heavy tribute of silver, wheat, and barley for three consecutive years.
options.note: Verse 5 records this victory and the resulting three years of substantial tribute.

options.text: He was defeated by the Ammonites and forced to pay them tribute instead.
options.note: The text records the opposite outcome, with the Ammonites paying tribute to Jotham after his victory over them.

options.text: He formed a peaceful alliance with the Ammonites with no military conflict.
options.note: The text explicitly describes Jotham waging war against and defeating the Ammonites, not a peaceful alliance.

options.text: No military action is described in the chapter.
options.note: Verse 5 explicitly describes this military victory and its results.

quiz.q: According to the chronicler, why did Jotham grow powerful?

options.text: Because he ordered his ways before the LORD his God.
options.note: Verse 6 gives this exact, compact explanation for his strength.

options.text: Because he formed a military alliance with Israel to the north.
options.note: The text attributes his strength to his ordered devotion to the LORD, not a political alliance with Israel.

options.text: Because he inherited an already overwhelming army from his father.
options.note: The specific reason given is his own ordered walk before God, not merely an inherited military advantage.

options.text: The text gives no explanation for his strength.
options.note: Verse 6 explicitly gives this reason for his growing power.

questions: Jotham learned a specific, costly lesson from his father's failure rather than repeating it. What mistake in your own family or past have you consciously chosen not to repeat, and how has that shaped you?

The chronicler describes Jotham's strength in one simple phrase, he ordered his ways before the LORD. What would it look like to order your own daily ways, your schedule, decisions, and habits, more deliberately before God this week?

Even under a good king, the people still behaved corruptly. Where do you see the limits of good leadership alone to change hearts, and what does this teach you about needing personal, not just corporate, faithfulness?

Jotham's reign was quiet and largely without dramatic incident, yet the chronicler commends it. Where might God be honoring a quiet, undramatic season of faithfulness in your own life that feels unremarkable to you?

Jotham respected a boundary his father had violated at great cost. Is there a boundary, in your work, relationships, or spiritual life, that you know you should respect but have been tempted to cross because of past success or confidence?

translationNotes.note: A hundred talents of silver is approximately 3.77 tons or 3.42 metric tons; ten thousand cors of wheat and of barley each represent roughly 62,400 bushels, an enormous annual tribute reflecting Jotham's genuine strength during this period.

Ordered his ways translates a form of the Hebrew kun, elsewhere used of establishing or making secure a throne or foundation, suggesting Jotham's careful, deliberate faithfulness is what gave his reign real stability.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 27:1-9. No em dashes or en dashes appeared in the source text for this chapter.
